EVENING ROUNDUP — Three stories shaping the week across the Loran Valley, from the halls of the provincial assembly to a moonlit field outside Dell.

BUDGET VOTE STALLS. The provincial assembly adjourned for a third straight day on Thursday without passing its annual budget, as members from the northern districts refused to back a plan they said shortchanged rural road repair. The finance minister told reporters that talks would continue over the weekend and insisted a deal was "within reach," though several backbenchers left the chamber saying the two sides remained far apart on funding for bridges and culverts damaged in last winter's floods. No new vote has been scheduled. Analysts warned that a prolonged deadlock could delay payments to contractors already at work on the valley's highways.

BALLOON FESTIVAL LIFTS OFF. Meanwhile, on the broad grass meadow outside the village of Dell, more than sixty hot-air balloons rose together at dawn on Friday to open the valley's annual balloon festival, filling the pale sky with globes of crimson, gold, and striped blue. Pilots inflated their envelopes in the dark, and as the sun crested the eastern ridge the burners roared and the balloons lifted almost as one, drifting north over the vineyards while thousands of spectators watched from the meadow below. A balloon shaped like an enormous green turtle drew the loudest cheers from the children lining the fence. The festival runs through Sunday, weather permitting, with evening "night glow" displays in which tethered balloons light up in time to music.

DAIRY PRICES EASE. In economic news, the valley dairy cooperative announced that the wholesale price of milk had fallen for the second month running, a modest relief for the region's cheesemakers after a costly spring. The cooperative's director attributed the decline to a strong grass season and lower feed costs, and said he expected prices to remain stable through the summer. Retail prices, he cautioned, would take longer to follow.

The assembly returns Monday. The balloons fly again at dawn.
